items.prompt_md: Why does in-place reversal need three pointers (`prev`, `curr`, `nxt`) instead of two?
options: Because `nxt` saves the rest of the list before `curr.next` is overwritten, so nothing is stranded
Because Python requires exactly three loop variables for any while loop
Because linked lists always need one pointer per node in the list
Because `prev` and `curr` alone can traverse the list, and `nxt` is only used for printing
items.answer: Because `nxt` saves the rest of the list before `curr.next` is overwritten, so nothing is stranded
items.explanation_md: The moment `curr.next = prev` runs, the original forward link is gone — `nxt` is what lets the walk continue past that point.

items.prompt_md: A teammate writes the reversal loop as `curr.next = prev; prev, curr = curr, nxt` but forgets to compute `nxt = curr.next` first. What happens?
options: Nothing changes — the order doesn't matter
The list reverses twice as fast
The rest of the original list is lost, because `curr.next` was overwritten before its old value was read
It raises an exception immediately
items.answer: The rest of the original list is lost, because `curr.next` was overwritten before its old value was read
items.explanation_md: Once `curr.next` points at `prev`, the link to the remaining nodes is gone; without saving it in `nxt` first, the walk cannot continue and everything after `curr` becomes unreachable.

items.prompt_md: Why does building a brand-new reversed list (allocating a fresh node for each value) fall short of what most reversal problems ask for?
options: It's actually faster than the in-place version
It's O(n) time but also O(n) extra space, and the problem usually wants the existing nodes rewired, not copied
It doesn't work for lists longer than 1000 nodes
It can't handle an empty list
items.answer: It's O(n) time but also O(n) extra space, and the problem usually wants the existing nodes rewired, not copied
items.explanation_md: Copying preserves correctness but spends memory the in-place three-pointer sweep never needs, and it violates the common constraint of reusing the given nodes.

items.prompt_md: After the reversal loop `while curr: ...` finishes, why is `prev` (not `curr`) the new head?
options: Because `curr` becomes `None` when the loop exits, while `prev` holds the last real node visited, which is now first
Because `prev` was the original head all along and never changed
Because the loop always swaps `prev` and `curr` an even number of times
It's arbitrary — either pointer would work
items.answer: Because `curr` becomes `None` when the loop exits, while `prev` holds the last real node visited, which is now first
items.explanation_md: The loop runs until `curr` walks off the end of the list and becomes `None`; `prev` is left one step behind, sitting on the node that used to be the tail — now the reversed list's head.

items.prompt_md: Why does reversing a list recursively (`reverse_list(head.next)` then rewiring) still need the same core insight as the iterative three-pointer version?
options: It doesn't — recursion avoids pointer flipping entirely
It still has to flip each node's `next` pointer backward exactly once, per node, before the old forward link is lost — the call stack just plays the role `prev` would have
Recursive reversal is always O(n^2)
Recursion only works if the list has an even number of nodes
items.answer: It still has to flip each node's `next` pointer backward exactly once, per node, before the old forward link is lost — the call stack just plays the role `prev` would have
items.explanation_md: The recursive version delays the pointer flip until unwinding, but the underlying rule is identical: touch each node once, direct its link the other way, don't lose the rest of the list.

items.prompt_md: What edge case must the reversal loop's guard `while curr:` correctly handle without special-casing?
options: A list with negative values
An empty list (`head is None`), where the loop body never runs and `prev` (still `None`) is correctly returned
A list where every value is the same
A list with exactly one million nodes
items.answer: An empty list (`head is None`), where the loop body never runs and `prev` (still `None`) is correctly returned
items.explanation_md: When `head` is `None`, `curr` starts as `None`, the `while curr` guard is immediately false, and the function returns `prev`, which is still `None` — the correct reversal of an empty list, with zero special-casing.

items.prompt_md: Why is a linked list a poor fit for the "copy into an array, read backward" reversal trick that works fine for arrays?
options: It works exactly as well as it does for arrays
It costs O(n) extra space and still requires rewriting every node's `next` pointer afterward to actually reverse the list structure itself, not just the order you read it in
Linked lists cannot be copied into arrays at all
Arrays can't be reversed either, so the comparison doesn't apply
items.answer: It costs O(n) extra space and still requires rewriting every node's `next` pointer afterward to actually reverse the list structure itself, not just the order you read it in
items.explanation_md: Reading values backward from an array copy gives you the reversed *values*, but the problem usually wants the actual node links reversed — that copy doesn't save the in-place pointer rewiring step at all.

items.prompt_md: In `prev, curr = curr, nxt`, what hazard does the simultaneous (tuple) assignment avoid compared to writing it as two separate lines in the wrong order?
options: It doesn't matter, either order works
Writing `curr = nxt` before `prev = curr` would overwrite `curr` before its old value is used to update `prev`, corrupting the walk
Python requires tuple assignment for all loop variables
It changes the time complexity from O(n) to O(n^2)
items.answer: Writing `curr = nxt` before `prev = curr` would overwrite `curr` before its old value is used to update `prev`, corrupting the walk
items.explanation_md: The safe sequential order is `prev = curr` then `curr = nxt`; reversing that order overwrites `curr` before it's used to set `prev`, silently corrupting the walk — the tuple form sidesteps the ordering hazard entirely.

items.prompt_md: Why do problems like "reorder a list" or "check if a list is a palindrome" lean on the reversal pattern even though they aren't asking to reverse the whole list outright?
options: They don't — reversal is unrelated to those problems
Because they fold part of the list back on itself, which requires reversing a segment (often the second half) so it can be walked alongside the rest in the opposite direction
Because those problems always require sorting first
Because those problems can only be solved recursively
items.answer: Because they fold part of the list back on itself, which requires reversing a segment (often the second half) so it can be walked alongside the rest in the opposite direction
items.explanation_md: Reordering or palindrome-checking a list both need to compare or interleave a segment with its mirror image — reversing that segment in place is what makes the comparison a simple parallel walk.

items.prompt_md: Why is the in-place reversal pattern described as touching "every `next` pointer exactly once"?
options: Because the loop body runs once per node, and each iteration flips exactly one node's `next` pointer before advancing — no pointer is revisited
Because it actually visits each node twice, once to read and once to write
Because it only flips the first and last pointers, leaving the middle untouched
Because the number of flips depends on the values stored, not the node count
items.answer: Because the loop body runs once per node, and each iteration flips exactly one node's `next` pointer before advancing — no pointer is revisited
items.explanation_md: Each node is `curr` exactly once across the whole walk, and its `next` is rewritten exactly then — which is precisely what makes the whole pass O(n).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the three-pointer in-place reversal for a list of n nodes?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Each node is visited exactly once as `curr`, doing constant work (save `nxt`, flip `next`, advance both pointers) — n nodes, n constant-time steps, so O(n) overall.

items.prompt_md: What is the extra (auxiliary) space complexity of the three-pointer in-place reversal, not counting the input list itself?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: Only three pointer variables (`prev`, `curr`, `nxt`) are ever used, regardless of list length — no new nodes and no auxiliary array are allocated.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the "build a new reversed list by prepending freshly allocated nodes" approach, for a list of n nodes?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: It still visits every node once to read its value and allocate a corresponding new node — O(n) time, though at the cost of O(n) extra space that the in-place version avoids.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of copying every node's value into an array, reversing the array, and building a new list from it, for n nodes?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: The intermediate array holds all n values at once, so this trades away the O(1) extra space that in-place pointer reversal achieves.

items.prompt_md: Reversing only a sub-range `[left, right]` of an n-node list still costs what time complexity in the worst case (e.g., right is near the end)?
options: O(right - left)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Even though only the segment between `left` and `right` is rewired, reaching `left` in the first place can require walking up to n - 1 nodes, so the worst-case bound is still O(n).

items.prompt_md: A problem asks you to reverse a singly linked list in place, using no extra allocated nodes. Which approach fits?
options: Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
Fast & slow pointers
Copy nodes to an array first
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
items.explanation_md: "Reverse in place, no new nodes" is the direct trigger for the three-pointer sweep that flips each `next` link as it walks the list once.

items.prompt_md: You need to reverse only the nodes between position `left` and position `right` of a singly linked list, leaving the rest untouched. Which approach fits?
options: Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt, applied only within the bounded segment
Fast & slow pointers to find the middle first
Dummy head + merge
Copy the whole list to an array first
items.answer: Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt, applied only within the bounded segment
items.explanation_md: A bounded reversal is still the same three-pointer flip, just started and stopped at different nodes — no need to touch the segments outside `[left, right]`.

items.prompt_md: You must check whether a singly linked list reads the same forward and backward, without allocating O(n) extra nodes. Which approach fits?
options: Find the middle, reverse the second half in place, then compare the two halves node by node
Dummy head + merge
Hash set of visited nodes
Sort the values first
items.answer: Find the middle, reverse the second half in place, then compare the two halves node by node
items.explanation_md: Folding the list onto itself to compare halves needs the second half's direction flipped first — the in-place reversal trick applied to a segment rather than the whole list.

items.prompt_md: Given an array (not a linked list) of integers, you're asked to reverse it in place with O(1) extra space. Which approach best fits an ARRAY under this constraint?
options: Two pointers starting at each end, swapping and stepping inward until they meet
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
items.answer: Two pointers starting at each end, swapping and stepping inward until they meet
items.explanation_md: Trap: reversal sounds like the linked-list pattern, but an array has random-access indices and no `next` pointers to flip — swapping from both ends inward is the array-native tool.

items.prompt_md: You're given a string and must reverse the order of its characters in place (treating it as a mutable character array), using O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers starting at each end, swapping characters and stepping inward
Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
Fast & slow pointers
Copy nodes to an array first
items.answer: Two pointers starting at each end, swapping characters and stepping inward
items.explanation_md: Trap: "reverse" cues the linked-list pointer flip, but a string/character array has indices, not `next` pointers — the two-pointer inward swap is the right tool here, not a pointer-rewiring sweep.

items.prompt_md: You need to reverse the digits of an integer (e.g., 123 -> 321) without converting it to a linked list or array structure. Which approach fits best?
options: Repeatedly pop the last digit with `% 10` and `// 10`, building the result digit by digit
Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
items.answer: Repeatedly pop the last digit with `% 10` and `// 10`, building the result digit by digit
items.explanation_md: Trap: this is arithmetic on a number, not a pointer-based structure at all — there's no `next` field to flip, so neither linked-list reversal nor any pointer pattern applies.

items.prompt_md: You must reverse the words in a sentence (e.g., "the sky is blue" -> "blue is sky the") while keeping single spaces between words. Which approach fits?
options: Split into words, reverse the list of words, and join them back together
Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
items.answer: Split into words, reverse the list of words, and join them back together
items.explanation_md: Trap: "reverse" again points at the linked-list flip, but a sentence is a string/array of words with indices — this is array-style manipulation, not `next`-pointer rewiring.

items.prompt_md: You're implementing an undo stack where reversing recent operations means popping them off in last-in-first-out order, not rewiring any `next` fields. Which approach fits?
options: Use a stack (LIFO), popping the most recent operation each time
Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
items.answer: Use a stack (LIFO), popping the most recent operation each time
items.explanation_md: Trap: "reversing recent operations" sounds like list reversal, but there's no linked structure with `next` pointers to flip here — a LIFO stack (an array/hash-adjacent structure) is the natural tool.

items.prompt_md: You need to reverse a singly linked list recursively rather than iteratively, still rewiring the existing nodes with no new allocations. Which approach fits?
options: Recursively reverse the rest of the list first, then have the current node point its predecessor's `next` back at itself
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: Recursively reverse the rest of the list first, then have the current node point its predecessor's `next` back at itself
items.explanation_md: Recursive reversal is still the linked-list pointer-flip pattern — the call stack takes over the bookkeeping role that `prev` plays in the iterative version, but every node's `next` still gets flipped exactly once.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a singly linked list is a palindrome, but you're only allowed to reverse the SECOND half of the list (found via fast/slow pointers) rather than the whole list, to compare it against the first half. Which approach fits for that reversal step?
options: Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt, applied starting at the middle node
Copy nodes to an array first
Hash set of visited nodes
Dummy head + merge
items.answer: Reverse pointers in place with prev/curr/nxt, applied starting at the middle node
items.explanation_md: The reversal step is identical to the whole-list version, just started partway through — flip each `next` pointer in the second half backward using the same three-pointer sweep.
